coredata.dc:title: A new electrochemical biosensor based on telomeric G-quadruplex DNA: In silico and experimental study of dihydropyridine derivatives potential effect on telomerase inhibition
coredata.dc:creator: Aghaei, Roghayyeh
Mazloum-Ardakani, Mohammad
Abdollahi-Alibeik, Mohammad
Moshtaghioun, Seyed Mohammad
Rezaeipoor-Anari, Ali
Haghighijoo, Zahra
Zamani, Leila
coredata.dc:description: The electrochemical biosensor was prepared by modification of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with Au nanoparticles (AuNPs), G-quadruplex DNA (G4DNA) and i-motif DNA (C4DNA or i-DNA) structures to investigate of telomerase enzyme inhibitors. SH-G4DNA and SH-i-DNA were linked to the modified electrode by AuNPs–S binding. This platform was used to study the quadruplex DNA/ligand interaction. The interaction of different ligands with quadruplex DNA was studied in a 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution as a redox probe at the surface of these biosensors, using the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique. The resistance of charge transfer (Rct) values was increased by increasing the concentration of ligand due to the quadruplex DNA/ligand interaction. Under the optimum conditions, the observations revealed that 1,4-dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP) derivatives had a good affinity toward the quadruplex DNA structures in the concentration range of 5.0–700.0μM. The selectivity was examined using different double-stranded (ds-DNA) sequences.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Telomeric G-Quadruplex DNA
In silico
Au nanoparticles
Electrochemical biosensor
Dihydropyridine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The characterization of the modified electrode
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy studies at different stages of the quadruplex DNA biosensor preparation.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy study of the quadruplex DNA/ligand interaction at the electrode surface.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nyquist plots for the interaction of ligand A with GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA (concentration range 5μM–1000μM), GCE/AuNPs/SH-i-DNA (concentration range 5μM–600μM), and GCE/AuNPs/ds-DNA biosensors. ∆Rct binding curves for (■) G4DNA, (●) i-DNA and (▲) ds-DNA in a PBS (100mM, pH7.4, 0.9% NaCl) containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at scanning frequencies from 0.01 to 100,000Hz.

Nyquist plots for the interaction of ligand B with GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA (concentration range 5μM–1000μM), GCE/AuNPs/SH-i-DNA (concentration range 5μM–800μM), and GCE/AuNPs/ds-DNA biosensors. ∆Rct binding curves for (■) G4DNA, (●) i-DNA and (▲) ds-DNA in a PBS (100mM, pH7.4, 0.9% NaCl) containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at scanning frequencies from 0.01 to 100,000Hz.

Nyquist plots for interaction of ligand C with GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA (concentration range 5μM–800μM), GCE/AuNPs/SH-i-DNA (concentration range 5μM–300μM) and GCE/AuNPs/ds-DNA biosensors. ∆Rct binding curves for (■) G4DNA, (●) i-DNA and (▲) ds-DNA in a PBS (100mM, pH7.4, 0.9% NaCl) containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at scanning frequencies from 0.01 to 100,000Hz.

Nyquist plots for the interaction of ligand D with GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA (concentration range 5μM–1000μM), GCE/AuNPs/SH-i-DNA (concentration range 5μM–1000μM) and GCE/AuNPs/ds-DNA biosensors. ∆Rct binding curves for (■) G4DNA, (●) i-DNA and (▲) ds-DNA in a PBS (100mM, pH7.4, 0.9% NaCl) containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at scanning frequencies from 0.01 to 100,000Hz.

Nyquist plots for the interaction of ligand E with GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA (concentration range 5μM–1000μM), GCE/AuNPs/SH-i-DNA (concentration range 5μM–1000μM) and GCE/AuNPs/ds-DNA biosensors. ∆Rct binding curves for (■) G4DNA, (●) i-DNA and (▲) ds-DNA in a PBS (100mM, pH7.4, 0.9% NaCl) containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at scanning frequencies from 0.01 to 100,000Hz.

Nyquist plots for the interaction of ligand F with GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA (concentration range 5μM–1000μM), GCE/AuNPs/SH-i-DNA (concentration range 5μM–1000μM) and GCE/AuNPs/ds-DNA biosensors. ∆Rct binding curves for (■) G4DNA, (●) i-DNA and (▲) ds-DNA in a PBS (100mM, pH7.4, 0.9% NaCl) containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at scanning frequencies from 0.01 to 100,000Hz.
A–F: The binding mode of compounds A–F.
Schematic diagram of the fabrication of GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA/DHP biosensor.

head.ce:title: A new electrochemical biosensor based on telomeric G-quadruplex DNA: In silico and experimental study of dihydropyridine derivatives potential effect on telomerase inhibition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, Yazd 89195-741, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd 89195-741, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medicinal and Natural Products Chemistry Research Center, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences, Shiraz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Medicinal and Natural Products Chemistry Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mazandaran University of Medical Sciences, Sari, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Pharmacy and Pharmaceutical Sciences Research Center, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences, Shiraz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Pharmacy and Pharmaceutical Sciences Research Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electrochemical biosensor was prepared by modification of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with Au nanoparticles (AuNPs), G-quadruplex DNA (G4DNA) and i-motif DNA (C4DNA or i-DNA) structures to investigate of telomerase enzyme inhibitors. SH-G4DNA and SH-i-DNA were linked to the modified electrode by AuNPs–S binding. This platform was used to study the quadruplex DNA/ligand interaction. The interaction of different ligands with quadruplex DNA was studied in a 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution as a redox probe at the surface of these biosensors, using the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique. The resistance of charge transfer (Rct) values was increased by increasing the concentration of ligand due to the quadruplex DNA/ligand interaction. Under the optimum conditions, the observations revealed that 1,4-dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP) derivatives had a good affinity toward the quadruplex DNA structures in the concentration range of 5.0–700.0μM. The selectivity was examined using different double-stranded (ds-DNA) sequences.

Electrochemical biosensor based on telomeric G-Quadruplex DNA for investigation of telomerase enzyme inhibitors as a potential drug target by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.
Image 2

ce:section.ce:para: Telomeric DNA, while it is associated with the Shelterin nucleoprotein complex, can protect chromosomal ends from end-to-end fusions and consequently DNA damage and also the mentioned complex with the enzyme telomerase plays a pivotal role in telomere length regulation [1,2]. The telomerase actively extends the length of telomeric DNA by addition of d(TTAGGG) repeats to the 3′ terminus to ensure chromosomal integrity after each cell cycle. Under physiological concentrations of Na+ and K+, in vitro, guanine-rich DNA sequences can fold into stable four-stranded G-quadruplex structures. While motifs sequence folds into quadruplexes under the control of specific telomere binding proteins, they can be found in the telomeres [3,4]. G-quadruplex motifs have been identified throughout the genome and concentrate immediately upstream of transcription initiation sites [5].

The self-complementary nature of duplex DNA can cause the presence of approximately 370′000 C-rich motifs (C3+ N1–7)4+ (where C=cytosine and N=any base) in the human genome [6–8]. The mentioned C-rich sequences can fold into a type of tetraplex called ‘i-motif’ that contains hemi-protonated, intercalated C–C+ base pairs, under slightly acidic conditions (pH=6). For the best of our knowledge, almost nothing is known about the presence or biological relevance of i-motif DNA in vivo [7]. Thus, these results indicate that i-motifs might be formed under physiological conditions in vivo through molecular binding and/or crowding interactions [9].

As known, telomerase is up-regulated in 85% of human cancers, with minimal activity in somatic cells, making telomerase as an attractive target for therapeutic intervention in cancer. A number of methods have been developed for telomerase inhibition [10–13]. Up to now, many small molecules that bind to telomeric quadruplexes have been developed but none of them have been fully used as an anticancer therapeutics, due to low potency and selectivity. Unfortunately, in vivo antitumor data is mostly restricted to acridine-derived compounds and the natural product telomestatin. So, selection of a ligand with a good affinity toward G-quadruplex to stabilize or fold its structure is a good suggestion for cancer therapy. The designed ligands with high affinity with G-quadruplex could inhibit the telomerase activity, which is important in the treatment of cancers [14–19]. Choosing a ligand with a good affinity with G-quadruplex to stabilize or fold this structure is a good strategy for cancer therapy. This is because these methods could inhibit the telomerase activity, which is important in the treatment of cancers.

The compounds with 1,4-dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP) scaffold have received considerable attention due to their pharmacological activities. A literature survey revealed that these compounds have a variety of biological activities including antagonist [20], cardiovascular effects PAF-acether [21], neuroprotection properties [22], sirtuinactivators [23] and N-type calcium channel blockers [24]. Among the various 1,4-DHPs, polyhydroquinolines with 1,4-dihydropyridine pharmacophore in their structure have shown cardiovascular effect [25], antitumour and antidiabetic activities [26].

To evaluate the interaction properties of the ligand with a number of quadruplexes DNA targets, the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) has been used [27,28]. Recently, electrochemical approach has been paid more attention due to some properties such as low cost, the inherent specificity and high sensitivity for directly monitoring the interactions between drug and target compounds [29–34]. In addition, an electrochemical system can also act as a versatile and illuminating model for gaining insight into the in vivo action in living cells. The electrochemical approach is a useful technique for the quantitative analysis in pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids [33,35–37].

Many research articles using G-quadruplexes for various electrochemical applications such as bioassays, biosensor and chemical sensor have been published. For instance, fabrication of a sensitive electrochemical biosensor by modifying the screen-printed graphite electrode (SPE) with the synthesized SBA-N-propylpipyrazine-N-(2-mercaptopropane-1-one) (SBA@NPPNSH) mesoporous structures and Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) has been reported by Bagheryan [29]. The interaction of G4-DNAs and ds-DNAs with 5-fluoro-1-(arylsulfonyl) pyrimidine-2,4(1H,3H)-diones through electrochemical techniques, in which DNAs were coated onto a gold electrode has been reported by Quan [38].

Herein, the primary biological evaluation of 1,4-dihydropyridines as a new class of G-quadruplex stabilizing ligands is reported. The G-quadruplex stabilization potential of 1,4-dihydropyridines (A–F) was evaluated by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique measuring the shift in electron charge transfer resistance (ΔRct) of the folded G-quadruplex structures as a function of ligand concentration. Controlling DNA immobilization on the surfaces of nanoparticles is an essential step for the design and construction of efficient DNA-electrochemical biosensors.

In the present study, the primary biological evaluation of 1,4-dihydropyridines as a new class of G-quadruplex stabilizing ligands is reported. For this aim, a sensitive electrochemical method is suggested to investigate these molecules. A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified with the gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) by electrochemical method and tiolated-G4DNA strands. These modified biosensors were used in all experiments. Under the optimum conditions, the observations revealed that 1,4-dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP) derivatives indicated a good affinity toward the quadruplex DNA structures in the concentration range of 5.0–700.0μM. The G-quadruplex stabilization potential of 1,4-dihydropyridine derivatives was evaluated by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique measuring the shift in electron charge transfer resistance (ΔRct) of the folded G-quadruplex structures as a function of ligand concentration. The selectivity of the designed biosensors was examined using different double-stranded (ds-DNA) sequences. The detailed characterization of a probable binding model of 1,4-dihydropyridine derivatives in complexes of Human Telomeric DNA (PDB ID: 1KF1) structure was performed with docking simulation.

Specific thiol-terminated oligonucleotides and probes and were designed based on reference sequences obtained from the GenBank database. The oligonucleotides were designed and their secondary structure was examined with Gene Runner software (version 3.05, Hastings Software, USA). All the oligonucleotides were synthesized by Macrogen (Korea). The following designed synthetic oligonucleotide sequences were used:
SH-G4DNA: 5′SH-(CH2)6-GGGTTAGGGTTAGGGTTAGGGTTAGGG3′.
SH-C4DNA: 5′SH-(CH2)6-CCCTAACCCTAACCCTAACCCTAACCC3′.
G4DNA (complimentary): CCCTAACCCTAACCCTAACCCTAACCC.
C4DNA (complimentary): GGGTTAGGGTTAGGGTTAGGGTTAGGG.

The reagents have been prepared in deionized water and the solutions and glassware are autoclaved prior to use. HAuCl4 ∙3H2O (pro analysis grade), sulfuric acid (H2SO 4 ), Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), K4Fe(CN)6, K3Fe(CN)6 and other reagents were purchased from Merck Company (Darmstadt, Germany). 1,4-Dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP) derivatives were synthesized and characterized in our research group, and their structures are presented in the supporting information (Scheme S1). These chemical structures have been confirmed by means of 1H NMR, 13C NMR, IR, mass spectrometry, and elemental analysis [39]. 1,4-Dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP) derivatives were dissolved in a solution of ethanol and water (1/1 v/v).

The electrochemical measurements has been conducted on an IviumStat Potentiostat/Galvanostat using a three-electrode system with modified glassy carbon electrode as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and platinum as counter electrode, in a phosphate buffer solution (0.1M, pH7.4, 0.9% NaCl) containing 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) measurements were carried out with VEGA 3 SEM.

Before each experiment, the GCE was mechanically polished with 0.05μm alumina powder on micro cloth pads till a mirror-finish surface was obtained. The electrode was instantly rinsed thoroughly with deionized water, and outrightwashed successively with anhydrous alcohol and deionized water in an ultrasonic bath, respectively. The modification of electrode surface with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs/GCE) was performed by cyclic voltammetry scanning from a solution containing 0.01M Na2SO4, 0.01M H2SO4, and 1.0mM HAu(III)Cl4 3H2O at a scan rate of 60mV/s for five scans. NaNO3 was employed as a supporting electrolyte.

The immobilization of quadruplex DNA containing thiol-terminated probe G-quadruplex DNA (SH-G4DNA) and i-motif DNA (SH-C4DNA), onto GCE/AuNP electrodes were performed for biosensors fabrication. The oligonucleotide monolayer was generated by treating the GCE/AuNP electrode with a 5μM thiol-terminated oligonucleotide solution (15μl of SH-G4DNA or SH-C4DNA were cast on the surface of the GCE/AuNP electrode) in a Tris–HCl buffer (0.1M, pH7.4) for 90min in a humid chamber at room temperature to allow AuNPs–S bond formation. The fabrication of the GCE/AuNPs/quadruplex DNA biosensors was followed by washing it with a Tris–HCl buffer (pH7.4) solution to remove any unbound SH-G4DNA present on its surface. Then, the interaction of quadruplex DNA (G4DNA and C4DNA) with ligands on the working electrode surface was performed by immersing the GCE/AuNPs/quadruplex DNA biosensors in the ligands solution (DHP) to fabricate GCE/AuNPs/quadruplex DNA/DHP. The schematic representation of the biosensor with fabrication steps is demonstrated in Scheme 1 .

To place the dsDNA strands at the GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA biosensor, it is essential that the G4-DNA sequences are denatured at the electrode surface and consequently, the single strands are formed. For this purpose, the GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA was immersed in a buffer solution (50mM Tris–HCl, pH7.4) at 95°C for 5min, followed by cooling in the ice bath for about 10min [40]. This pretreatment leads to conversion of quadruplex DNA to single strands of DNA. In the next step, 10μl of the complementary sequences (5μM) which are denatured similar to probe DNA (pDNA), was dropped on the GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA sensor and placed in the incubator for 30min at 40°C to perform hybridization process completely. The fabricated biosensor in this step is called GCE/AuNPs/dsDNA. The GCE/AuNPs/dsDNA biosensor then was placed in the solutions containing the designed ligands (DHP) with different concentrations for 1h to form GCE/AuNPs/dsDNA/DHP. Each step of the surface modification was investigated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique. The fabrication processes of the designed biosensor are shown in Scheme S2.

The yellow tetrazolium MTT (3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) is reduced by metabolically active cells, in part by the action of dehydrogenase enzymes, to generate reducing equivalents such as NADH and NADPH. The resulting intracellular purple formazan can be solubilized and quantified by spectrophotometric means. MTT assay to determine cell toxicity for different concentrations of 1,4-dihydropyridine derivatives was performed. Human lung cancer carcinoma cell line (A 549) were cultured in a 96-well plate at 37°C, and exposed to varying concentrations (10–300μM) of 1,4-dihydropyridine derivatives for 24h. Cells treated with medium only served as a negative control group. After removing the supernatant of each well and washing twice by PBS, 20μl of MTT solution (5mg ml−1 in PBS) and 100μl of medium were then introduced. After incubation for another 4h, the resultant formazan crystals were dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (100μl) and the absorbance intensity measured by a microplate reader at 570nm (Spectra max 190, Molecular Devices, Inc. Sunnyvale, CA). All experiments were performed in triplicate, and the relative cell viability (%) was expressed as a percentage relative to the untreated control cells.

Molecular modeling studies were done using Autodock 4.2 and Auto Dock Tools 1.5.6 (ADT) with Lamarckian Genetic Algorithm (LGA) for the interaction model between Human Telomeric DNA (PDB ID: 1KF1) structure and ligands. The G-quadruplex was enclosed into 50×50×50 active site box with the grid points 0.375Å apart. All other parameters were default settings, the binding mode for each of the docking cases were considered from the lowest energy docked conformation and cluster analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The response of the prepared electrochemical biosensor was related to its physical morphology. The surface morphology plays a key role in the selective and sensitive detection of the target. The morphology of bare GCE electrode and AuNPs/GCE electrode was characterized by SEM and the results are displayed in Fig. S1 (panel A). As can be seen (Fig. S1, panel B), uniform distribution of the AuNPs has been created on the GCE electrode. This result is in good agreement with the results obtained from the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) method in a phosphate buffer solution (100mM, pH7.0, 0.9% NaCl) containing 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. As shown in Fig. S1 (panel C), a decrease in Rct value is observed after the electrodeposition of AuNPs on GCE due to the high conductivity of gold nanoparticles.

For increasing the sensitivity and selectivity of quadruplex DNA biosensors, experimental conditions including the effect of immobilization time of SH-G4DNA, the effect of SH-G4DNA concentration and hybridization for fabrication of dsDNA time were optimized. In order to increase the amount of immobilized SH-G4DNA and improve its DNA hybridization efficiency, the effect of the immobilization time of SH-G4DNA at the AuNP modified GCE was investigated. The results of this investigation are reported in Fig. S2 (panel A). The electron charge transfer resistance (Rct) was adopted as the measurement signal for all optimization tests. As the results show, Rct values are increased with the increase of the probe immobilization time up to about 90min and then be constant. This may be due to the full coverage of the electrode surface by the SH-G4DNA after 90min. A probe immobilization time of 90min, therefore, was selected in subsequent studies.

Effect of DNA probe concentration for the immobilization process was investigated. The outcomes of immobilization with different concentrations of SH-G4DNA from 0.5μM to 10μM are shown in Fig. S2 (panel B). By increasing the amount of SH-G4DNA (0.5μM to 5μM) Rctvalues are increased and then the Rct values will be stable due to full coverage of the active binding sites at the electrode surface. So, this concentration (5μM) was selected as the optimum value for pDNA concentration for all subsequent work.

Since the analytical performance of the nucleic acid is directly related to its ability to recognize the target oligonucleotide, it is essential to optimize the time required for the hybridization. The effect of target hybridization time was studied over the range of 10–50min. The influence of hybridization time on the impedance response of the system from hybridization of 5μ M target DNA with the 5μ M immobilized probe is shown in Fig. S2 (panel C). The Rct values are increased with increasing the hybridization time from 5 to 30min, and levels off thereafter. Consequently, a 30min hybridization time was chosen for the fabrication of a GCE/AuNPs/ds-DNA biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy studies at different stages of the quadruplex DNA biosensor preparation.
ce:section.ce:para: EIS is a useful technique to prove detailed information on the impedance changes of the electrode interface. In a typical impedance spectrum, a semicircle part at higher frequencies and a straight line at lower frequencies correspond to the electron transfer-limit process and diffusion-limit process, respectively. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) can be estimated by the semicircle diameter of the impedance spectra at high frequency [41].Three biosensors were fabricated in the optimal conditions with two thiol-terminated probes DNA strands (GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA, GCE/AuNPs/SH-i-DNA, and GCE/AuNPs/ds-DNA) and employed for interaction with ligands. EIS was applied to investigate the Rct of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as a redox probe at the surface of the designed biosensors. Fig. S3 presents the Nyquist plots obtained for these biosensors in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution. Fig. S3A illustrates the Nyquist plots obtained for the GCE/AuNPs/SH-G4DNA biosensor from the hybridization of target DNA (5μM) with the immobilized SH-G4DNA. The results reveal that the Rct value of GCE is decreased when AuNPs are an attachment to the GCE surface indicating an easy electronic transport at the electrode surface upon modification with AuNPs (curve a, Rct =80Ω). The subsequent modification of the AuNPs/GCE surface with the SH-G4DNA and hybridization of SH-G4DNA with complementary DNA cause an increase in the Rct values after each step (curves b and c, Rct =11,500 and 18,150Ω, respectively). These increases may be ascribed to increase in the double layer capacitance resulting from the electrostatic repulsion of negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox probe due to the accumulation of negative charges resulting from SH-G4DNA and complementary DNA. Another biosensor (GCE/AuNPs/SH-i-DNA) in the optimal conditions was fabricated, in a similar way for the designed synthetic i-motif sequence. The results are shown in Fig. S3B. The impedance spectra were recorded for this biosensor after SH-i-DNA immobilization (curve b, Rct =14,500Ω) and hybridization (curve c, Rct =21,750Ω) processes. The quadruplex structures of these two sequences (G4DNA and C4DNA) in various configurations are formed with different arrangements at the electrode surface. This fact can be assumed as a reason for the noticeable differences of Rct between the two designed biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy study of the quadruplex DNA/ligand interaction at the electrode surface.
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the interaction properties of 1,4-dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP) (A–F) with a number of DNA targets (G4DNA, C4DNA, and dsDNA). EIS technique was used to obtain concentration range of ligands for investigation of ligand affinity toward DNA targets. The EIS results (Figs. 1–6
) reveal that ligands A–F, bind to the various G-quadruplex DNA targets with concentration ranging from 5 to 1000μM. The change in specific electron charge transfer resistance (∆Rct) was adopted as the measurement signal. None of the ligands showed significant binding to the control duplex DNA.

All ligands which are tested in the present work, showed a preference for binding to the G4DNA, with 2-fold selectivity for G4DNA over i-DNA for ligands A-C (Figs. 1–3) and 2-fold selectivity for i-DNA over G4DNA for ligand D (Fig. 4). The discrimination between quadruplex DNA and duplex DNA has now been reported for a number of small molecule ligands, but the discrimination between distinct quadruplexes by a small molecule is observed to be a greater challenge. We envisage that in some parts the observed quadruplex discrimination must arise from the distinct loop topology and/or sequences exhibited for the quadruplexes under study. Our obtained results support the potential for small molecules to discriminate between quadruplexes. The results reveal that the DHP ligands are able to stabilize the structure of quadruplexes so, these ligands are applicable to act as telomerase inhibitors.

To confirm that the used ligands (DHP ligands) in this study are nontoxic, MTT cell proliferation assay was performed. Measurement of cell viability and proliferation forms the basis for numerous in vitro assays of a cell population's response to external factors. The reduction of tetrazolium salts is now widely accepted as a reliable way to examine cell proliferation. Results are shown in Fig. S4. The results reveal that the used ligands (DHP) do not show any poisonous effect in the concentration range of 10–300μM on the cultured cells. The inverted microscope images are shown in this figure indicating that there is no any apoptosis in these images (Fig. S5).

Molecular modeling studies were achieved to identify the binding poses of dihydropyridine derivatives with G-quadruplex. Test compounds A-E were docked into the Human Telomeric DNA (PDB ID: 1KF1) structure.

Fig. 7(A–F) indicated all compounds have an affinity for the active site of the target. Docking strongly suggested that the polar contact between primary, secondary amines and acarbonyl group in the main scaffold with G-quadruplex, and also hydroxy and methoxy-substituted at the phenyl ring. The polar group of OH and OCH3 in the para and meta positions of the phenyl ring in A and B could participate in polar interaction well. Other substitutes may facilitate the suitable orientation of backbone in the effective mode to DNA structure. As you saw in C and E the methyl (rich electron density) and nitro (poor electron density) group respectively may push amines of backbone to polar orientation with telomeric DNA structure and the pyridinyl and phenyl substituted with D and F have the same direction in contact with primary amine and the carbonyl group of the ester.

The AuNP modified GCE was utilized as an effective nanostructure platform for constructing a quadruplex DNA biosensor and studying the interaction between a number of different 1,4-dihydropyridine (DHP) derivatives and the quadruplex DNA strands. Electrochemical studies confirmed that the designed biosensors provide a convenient and selective approach for investigation of the interaction between quadruplex DNA, quadruplex-binding ligand, and duplex DNA strands. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy technique was used for investigation of the interaction of quadruplex DNA/DHP. The observations indicated that DHP derivatives can stabilize quadruplex DNA structures and docking study could determine the interaction of compounds A–F with G-quadruplex well; it was proposed the difference substituted on phenyl group of structures could affect the orientation. The main advantages of the suggested biosensors are rapid detection of the quadruplex DNA-binding ligands. Reports from our laboratory are in progress toward the usage of these biosensors for further investigation of the quadruplex DNA binding ligand potential as well as the determination of human telomeric DNA changes in the presence of positive and negative G4DNA-binding ligands and K+ ion. Also, this biosensor can be applicable as a sensitive and potent approach for screening antitumor activity of the new ligands and drugs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the Yazd University Research Council, IUT Research Council and Excellence in Sensors for financial support of this research.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2017.04.055.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Shelterin: the protein complex that shapes and safeguards human telomeres
How shelterin solves the telomere end-protection problem
Cold Spring Harb. Symp. Quant. Biol.
Crystal structure of parallel quadruplexes from human telomeric DNA
Propeller-type parallel-stranded G-quadruplexes in the human c-myc promoter
Telomere end-binding proteins control the formation of G-quadruplex DNA structures in vivo
Prevalence of quadruplexes in the human genome
Carboxyl-modified single-walled carbon nanotubes selectively induce human telomeric i-motif formation
Highly prevalent putative quadruplex sequence motifs in human DNA
Duplex dissociation of telomere DNAs induced by molecular crowding
Electrochemical and circular dichroism spectroscopic evidence of two types of interaction between [Ru(NH3)6]3+ and an elongated thrombin binding aptamer G-quadruplex
A water-soluble tetraazaperopyrene dye as strong G-quadruplex DNA binder
Highly sensitive electrochemiluminescent DNA biosensor based on hydrazide-modified graphene quantum dots and hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme
A G-quadruplex aptamer based impedimetric sensor for free lysine and arginine
